Homogeneity --- Introduction ============ Let $\mathcal{M}=(M,g)$ be a pseudo-Riemannian manifold of dimension $m\ge2$. Let $
abla^kR\in\otimes^{k+4}T^*M$ denote the $k^{\operatorname{th}}$ covariant derivative of the curvature tensor $R$. Let $
abla^k\mathcal{R}:=(R,
abla R,\dots,
abla^kR)$ be the associated $k$-curvature model. Let $\mathcal{I}$ be the group of isometries of $\mathcal{M}$ and let $\mathcal{H}$ be the group of homotheties of $\mathcal{M}$; $\mathcal{I}$ is the isotropy group of the group $\mathcal{H}$ and $\mathcal{H}$ is the homotheties group of the isotropy group $\mathcal{I}$. The manifold $\mathcal{M}$ is said to be $k$-curvature homogeneous if for every $P,Q\in M$, there is an isometry $\Phi:T_PMightarrow T_QM$ so $\Phi^*
abla_Q^k\mathcal{R}=
abla_P^k\mathcal{R}$. Similarly, $\mathcal{M}$ is said to be $k$-homothety curvature homogeneous if for every $P,Q\in M$ there is a homothety $\Phi$ and a constant $C$ so $\Phi^*
abla_Q^k\mathcal{R}=C
abla_P^k\mathcal{R}$. These notions were introduced and studied by Kowalski and Vanžurová and arose from the purely homogeneous case $k$=0 by Kowalski, Tricerri, Vanhecke, and Vanžurová and the purely homogeneous case by Kowalski, Tricerri, and Vanžurová. texts.full_text: transmission of HIV-1 (MTCT) in a resource-limited setting. This prospective open-label, randomized 4-arm study (May 1999 to May 2000) conducted in South Africa enrolled 373 women from 34 weeks of gestation; medication was continued through delivery and for 6 weeks to infants. MTCT rates were ascertained at birth, 6, 12, and 24 weeks of age. Mean maternal HIV-1 RNA levels decreased rapidly on treatment in all groups. At week 4, the mean decrease was 1.91 log10copies/mL. The study aimed to evaluate the effectiveness of the four treatment regimens in reducing the risk of HIV-1 transmission from mother to child. The primary endpoint was the transmission of HIV-1 to the infant, as measured by HIV-1 DNA PCR in the infant's blood. The study also aimed to assess the safety of the regimens in mother and infant, and to determine the impact of the regimens on maternal HIV-1 RNA levels and on infant's HIV-1 DNA PCR results. During the study, the mother's HIV-1 RNA levels, a marker of viral load, decreased in all regimens, indicating the antiviral activity of the nucleoside analogues. However, the specific rates and patterns of maternal viral load suppression, and the corresponding rates of HIV-1 transmission, were the key focuses of the analysis. The transmission rates were evaluated at birth, and at 6, 12, and 24 weeks of the infant's life, to ensure the absence of late transmission. The maternal viral load at the 4 weeks of the regimen's initiation was a key predictor of the transmission rates, as was the viral load at birth and at the time of the infant's birth. texts.full_text: na Crest Viewpoint. A beautiful, spectacular spot, 800 feet above the river, easily accessible on my daily commute from Hood river to The Dalles. Looking upriver at the Columbia as it enters the eastern Gorge: a landscape defined and shaped by geology that predates humans by millions of years, and which will be here for millennia to come. Formed by massive lava flows millions of years ago, and subsequently carved out over multiple ice ages by incredible forces of nature, this viewpoint offers an awe-inspiring glimpse into the Earth's past. The sheer scale of the geological features here is humbling, reminding us of the vastness of time and the relentless power of natural processes. From Rowena Crest, you can see the Columbia River winding its way through a rugged landscape of basalt cliffs, deep gorges, and verdant forests. The river itself is a vital artery, supporting an ecosystem rich in biodiversity and serving as a lifeline for both people and wildlife in the region. On clear days, the view stretches for miles, offering panoramic vistas that capture the essence of the Pacific Northwest's wild beauty. Whether you're a photographer seeking the perfect shot or simply a traveler in search of inspiration, Rowena Crest Viewpoint is an unforgettable destination. As I stand here, gazing out at this ancient landscape, I am struck by a sense of continuity and connection—a link between past, present, and future that transcends time. It's a reminder of the enduring power of nature and the importance of preserving these natural wonders for generations

texts.full_text: r 4, 2015, Plaintiff filed a “due process complaint” against SSD with the Missouri State Board of Education and requested a hearing, as provided for under the IDEA, 20 U.S.C. § 1415(b)(1), (2) and (f). The matter was referred to the AHC, pursuant to state law. Following a pre-hearing conference call in December 2015, SSD and Hazelwood evaluated R.M. in February 2016 and determined that she was eligible for special education services (not including occupational or physical therapy). SSD moved to dismiss Plaintiff's complaint before the AHC, asserting that Plaintiff's claims were mooted by SSD's February 2016 evaluation and determination. The AHC granted SSD's motion and dismissed Plaintiff's due process complaint as moot on March 4, 2016. Plaintiff alleges that the AHC “colluded with representatives of SSD and/or their attorneys in an effort to give Hazelwood and SSD time to cover up their improper denial of [Plaintiff's] requests for an IEP and 504 evaluations for R.M, ” and that “AHC staff and/or their representatives altered the transcript of the pre-hearing conference call to give the appearance that [Plaintiff] had agreed to the actions taken by the [AHC], Hazelwood and SSD following the pre-hearing conference call.” ECF No. 48 at 11. Plaintiff did not appeal the dismissal of her due process complaint, as provided for by the IDEA, § 1415(g), but rather initiated this action pro se on March 8, 2016. In her three-count amended complaint prepared with the assistance of appointed counsel, Plaintiff asserts claims on her own behalf and on behalf of R.M. Genesis 1:31. 1. When God created the heavens and the earth, and all that is therein, at the conclusion of each day's work it is said, "And God saw that it was good." Whatever was created was good in its kind; suited to the end for which it was designed; adapted to promote the good of the whole and the glory of the great Creator. This sentence it pleased God to pass with regard to each particular creature. But there is a remarkable variation of the expression, with regard to all the parts of the universe, taken in connection with each other, and constituting one system: "And God saw everything that he had made, and, behold, it was very good."

texts.full_text: e because their brains are trying too hard to figure out what exactly is happening when really all you had was some cool idea that sounded great at first until actually put down on paper. This can often happen if your story or article has an interesting premise but doesn't have enough meat behind it; meaning not only does the basic structure of events need to make sense, so do any subplots and side stories within them as well. For example: in our restaurant scene above let's say there was another character who came into play during lunch - perhaps a waiter/waitress. If this other person is introduced out-of-the-blue with no explanation for how they fit into the story then it will feel forced; like you're trying too hard to add something extra just because "it would be cool." The best way around that? Introduce them earlier on before their scene so we know who these people are and why exactly we should care about what happens next when involving said characters. Or if introducing someone new after-the-fact, give us enough information beforehand (or even during) to help explain how this person fits into the larger picture. Pacing is another crucial part of any good story or article; it's basically just referring to how fast events happen and whether they feel rushed/forced/slowed down etc

texts.full_text: explores the relationship between masculinity and violence within the context of cultural change and escalating violence. This unique analysis links the growing sociological and psychological literature on masculinity with contemporary criminological research. The author focuses on: - A critical examination of the major biological, psychological, sociological, and anthropological models of masculinity and violence - Formulating an integrated theoretical approach to the relationship between violence and masculinity - Violence as a gendered activity - Representations of violence and masculinity in popular culture including cartoons, fiction, television and film. Masculine identity is not viewed as rigid, but as flexible and changeable. This position enables the author to take a completely fresh look at relations between power, privilege, and gender.This book explores the relationship between masculinity and violence within the context of cultural change and escalatingviolence. This text will be essential reading for students taking courses in Criminology or Sociology who are interested specifically with crime theory a

texts.full_text: Summer lovers were asked to upload their best photos at the beach with the hashtag #gsinstacontest. Whether alone or with friends, at the beach or by the pool, professionaal or amateur, everyone was encouraged to participate. Participants could win a chance to travel to one of the world's most beautiful beach destinations, courtesy of GS. The competition started with great enthusiasm, with people from all over the world participating. texts.full_text: an estimated 1,750 wild horses in Northern California in 2013, according to the bureau of Land Management. There are also a small population of grey wolves In California that inhabit Lassen, Siskiyou and Plumas Counties. Sierra Nevada Mammals of the Canadian zone include the mountain weasel, snowshoe hare, Sierra chickaree, and several species of chipmunk. Conspicuous birds include the blue-fronted jay, Sierra hermit thrush, water ouzel, and Townsend solitaire. Birds become scarcer as one ascends to the Hudsonian zone, and the wolverine is now regarded as rare. Only one bird is native to the high Arctic region—the Sierra rosy finch—but others often visit, including the hummingbird and Clark nutcracker. Principal mammals of this region are also visitors from other zones; the Sierra coney and white-tailed jackrabbit make their homes here. The bighorn sheep also lives in this mountainous terrain; the bighorn sheep was listed as endangered by the US Fish and Wildlife Service. Some animals in the Yosemite Valley include bobcats, mountain lions, ring-tailed cats, the Steller's jay, California ground squirrels and the American black bear. Lake Tahoe In terms of volume, the largest lake in California is Lake Tahoe, located on the California–Nevada border. The area in and around Lake Tahoe is filled with a huge diversity of animals and plants. Many mammals call the Lake Tahoe area home. Included among these are the yellow-bellied marmot, the Douglas squirrel or chickaree, golden-mantled ground squirrel, American martin, mule deer, black bear, coyote, raccoon, beaver, and porcupine. Birds in the region include American robin, bald eagle, California gull, Canada goose, dark-eyed junco, hairy woodpecker, mallard, mountain chickadee, red-tailed hawk, Steller's jay, western tanager, and yellow-headed blackbird. The region has numerous venomous spiders, such as the black widow, brown recluse, tarantula, and hobo

JNJ-38158471 inhibits VEGFR-2 (IC50 = 40 nM) and closely related tyrosine kinases, Ret (180 nM) and Kit (500 nM); it has no significant activity (>1 microM) against VEGFR-1 and VEGFR-3. texts.full_text: in preventing cancer development by regulating cell cycle progression, DNA repair, and apoptosis. When functioning properly, p53 helps to maintain genomic integrity; however, when mutated or dysregulated, it can lead to uncontrolled cell growth and proliferation, contributing to tumor formation and disease progression. Given the prevalence of p53 pathway abnormalities in NSCLC, gene therapy approaches targeting this pathway have been explored as potential therapeutic strategies. The aim of these therapies is to restore normal p53 function or bypass the dysfunctional pathway altogether by introducing a functional copy of the TP53 gene into cancer cells, using viral vectors for delivery, or by inhibiting molecules that contribute to the suppression of wild-type p53 activity. Additionally, several small molecule drugs have been developed to reactivate mutant p53 or to modulate the activity of other proteins within the pathway. Clinical trials investigating these gene therapy strategies in NSCLC have shown some promise. For instance, the introduction of a functional TP53 gene via adenoviral vectors has demonstrated efficacy in preclinical models and early-phase clinical studies. Similarly, small molecule drugs targeting the p53 pathway have shown activity in phase I/II trials, with some agents progressing to larger scale phase III trials based on their initial results. Despite these advances, several challenges remain in developing effective gene therapy strategies for NSCLC related to the p53 pathway. These include issues such as low efficiency of gene delivery to tumor cells, potential off-target effects, and resistance mechanisms that can limit the effectiveness of therapeutic interventions. We next meet the sworn enemy of Thebes, Prince Creonte of ThEphesus, who is seeking revenge against Anfione for past grievances. He plans a cunning plot to overthrow the city of Thebes, starting by manipulating the citizens through fear and deceit. In Act Two, Niobe's pride is tested as she becomes increasingly obsessed with her own beauty and the worship of her children. She spurns the warnings of the Oracle, who prophesies that her arrogance will lead to disaster. Meanwhile, Creonte's scheme begins to unfold, sowing discord among the Theban citizens and driving them to distrust their own leaders. As tension rises, Clearte struggles with his feelings for Niobe while attempting to maintain order as Regent. Tiresia, aware of the impending doom through his visions, urges his daughter Manto to seek out a solution to save Thebes. Manto, inspired by her father's wisdom and her own growing powers, sets off on a quest to find the legendary Golden Fleece, said to have the power to heal any wound and bring prosperity. The climax of the opera sees Manto's return with the Golden Fleece, but it's too late to save Thebes from the chaos that has already been unleashed.
